topics.overview: Questions ask you to explain price changes, shifts in demand or supply, and calculate/identify equilibrium. Diagrams are almost always rewarded.
keyPhrases: ceteris paribus
inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded
direct relationship between price and quantity supplied
shift of the demand/supply curve
movement along the curve
excess demand / excess supply
market clears at equilibrium
fullMarks: State the law clearly and precisely (e.g. 'As price rises, quantity demanded falls, ceteris paribus')
Identify the cause of the shift and name the factor (income, tastes, prices of related goods, expectations)
Draw a fully labelled demand/supply diagram showing the shift and new equilibrium
Explain the effect on both price and quantity
Give a specific, relevant real-world example
partialMarks: Correct law stated but no diagram or explanation
Correct diagram drawn but not labelled (axes, curves, equilibrium point)
Explanation of direction without stating reason
commonMistakes: Confusing a shift of the demand curve with a movement along it — a price change causes movement along the curve, not a shift
Saying 'demand increases because price falls' — this is the wrong causality; demand increases due to non-price factors
Unlabelled or incorrectly labelled axes on diagrams
Confusing 'demand' (the whole curve) with 'quantity demanded' (a point on the curve)
topics.examTip: Any question on demand or supply almost always awards marks for a labelled diagram. Always draw one, even if not explicitly asked. Label axes (Price/Quantity), both curves, the original and new equilibrium.

topics.overview: Examiner wants you to define PED precisely, calculate it correctly, classify whether demand is elastic/inelastic, and explain implications for revenue.
keyPhrases: responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price
% change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price
PED > 1 = elastic (price sensitive)
PED < 1 = inelastic (price insensitive)
total revenue rises/falls with a price change
availability of substitutes
proportion of income spent on the good
luxury vs necessity
fullMarks: Precise formula: PED = % ΔQd ÷ % ΔP
Correct numerical calculation showing working
Correct classification as elastic or inelastic with reference to the calculated value
Explanation of the revenue implication (elastic: price up → revenue falls; inelastic: price up → revenue rises)
State a factor that determines PED and explain why (e.g. 'many substitutes → more elastic')
partialMarks: Correct formula stated but calculation error
Correct answer without showing working
Definition without formula
commonMistakes: Getting the formula upside down (ΔP ÷ ΔQd instead of ΔQd ÷ ΔP)
Ignoring the negative sign — PED is always negative for normal goods; marks may be lost if sign is omitted where required
Confusing PED with YED or XED in multi-part questions
Saying 'elastic' without referring to the numerical value being > 1
topics.examTip: Always show the formula, then substitute values, then state whether elastic/inelastic. Two-mark questions usually want: formula + value. Four-mark questions also want: classification + revenue implication.

topics.overview: Questions focus on the Equi-Marginal Principle and the Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility. Numerical calculations testing MU/P ratios are very common.
keyPhrases: satisfaction derived from consuming a good
marginal utility decreases as consumption increases
consumer equilibrium: MU₁/P₁ = MU₂/P₂
utility is maximised when MU per euro is equal for all goods
allocate spending to equalise marginal utility per euro
fullMarks: State the Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility with an example
State the Equi-Marginal Principle formula: MU_A/P_A = MU_B/P_B
Set up and solve the calculation correctly, showing each MU/P ratio
Conclude which good to buy more/less of and why
Link back to utility maximisation
partialMarks: Correct principle stated but calculation not attempted
Correct MU/P ratio for one good only
Correct calculation but wrong conclusion
commonMistakes: Forgetting to divide MU by price — comparing raw MU values instead of MU/P
Getting the direction of adjustment wrong (should buy more of the good with higher MU/P)
Not stating units — utils per euro must be compared
topics.examTip: Calculation questions almost always use MU/P = MU/P. Show both ratios, state which is higher, state what the consumer should do to restore equilibrium. Full marks usually require the conclusion.

topics.overview: Examiner tests the Law of Diminishing Returns, types of costs (fixed/variable/marginal), and economies/diseconomies of scale. Short-run vs long-run distinction is important.
keyPhrases: short run: at least one fixed factor
long run: all factors variable
law of diminishing returns applies in the short run
as variable inputs increase, marginal product eventually falls
fixed costs do not change with output
average cost = total cost ÷ output
marginal cost = change in total cost ÷ change in output
economies of scale reduce long-run average cost
fullMarks: State the Law of Diminishing Returns precisely, mentioning fixed and variable factors
Distinguish clearly between short run and long run
Correctly define and calculate TC, AC, MC, TFC, TVC from a table
Name and explain at least two internal economies of scale with examples
Explain why diseconomies of scale occur with a specific example (e.g. communication breakdown)
partialMarks: Correct definition without example
Correct calculation of one cost measure but errors in others
Economies of scale listed but not explained
commonMistakes: Applying the Law of Diminishing Returns to the long run — it only applies in the short run
Confusing average cost (AC) with marginal cost (MC)
Saying fixed costs are zero — they are constant, not zero
topics.examTip: Table questions: always calculate MC before AC. When the question asks you to 'explain' an economy of scale, you must name it, define it, and give a specific example — three separate elements.

topics.overview: Examiner expects you to compare features of different market structures and analyse firm behaviour. Essay-style questions require comparison across structures.
keyPhrases: many buyers and sellers (perfect competition)
homogeneous product / price taker
freedom of entry and exit
single seller / price maker (monopoly)
barriers to entry
interdependence (oligopoly)
non-price competition
supernormal profit in short run
normal profit in long run (perfect competition)
concentration ratio
kinked demand curve (oligopoly)
fullMarks: Name all four features of the structure being asked about
Explain each feature with a sentence — not just list them
For comparison questions: explicitly compare named structures across each feature
Explain short-run vs long-run profit for perfect competition
Give a real Irish or international example of each market structure
partialMarks: Features listed without explanation
Correct features but no example
Only 2 of 4 features identified
commonMistakes: Saying 'no competition' for monopoly — a monopolist still faces some indirect competition
Confusing oligopoly with monopolistic competition — oligopoly has interdependence and few firms
Not mentioning barriers to entry for monopoly questions
Forgetting that in perfect competition only normal profit is earned in the long run due to freedom of entry
topics.examTip: A 16-mark market structures question almost always rewards: 4 features × 2 marks each + example + diagram. The diagram for perfect competition must show price = MC = AC at the minimum of AC in long run.

topics.overview: Examiner tests conditions necessary for price discrimination and its effects on consumers/producers. Must explain why different prices can be charged.
keyPhrases: same good sold at different prices to different consumers
not based on cost differences
market must be segmentable — markets must be kept separate
different price elasticities in each market
charge higher price in inelastic market
charge lower price in elastic market
increases producer surplus / reduces consumer surplus
fullMarks: Define price discrimination precisely
State three conditions necessary: monopoly power, separable markets, different PED in each market
Explain that higher price is charged where demand is more inelastic
Give a real-world example with two different prices clearly identified
Evaluate effect on consumers and producer
partialMarks: Definition only with no conditions
Example given but conditions not explained
Conditions listed without explaining why they are necessary
commonMistakes: Not stating that markets must be kept separate (arbitrage must be prevented)
Saying the monopolist can 'charge any price' — price depends on demand in each segment
Giving an example without identifying the two price segments
topics.examTip: The three conditions are the core of any price discrimination question. Memorise: (1) price-setting power, (2) separable markets, (3) different PED values. Examiners award up to 3 marks for conditions alone.

topics.overview: Examiner wants the definition and types of externality, a diagram showing divergence between private and social cost/benefit, and government policy responses.
keyPhrases: cost or benefit falling on third parties
private cost vs social cost
marginal private cost (MPC) vs marginal social cost (MSC)
deadweight welfare loss
overproduction at market equilibrium (negative externality)
underproduction (positive externality)
government intervention to internalise the externality
Pigouvian tax / subsidy
fullMarks: Define externality precisely — cost or benefit on third parties not reflected in the market price
Distinguish between positive and negative externality with an example of each
Draw a correctly labelled diagram: MPC, MSC (or MPB, MSB), showing the market output vs optimal output and the deadweight loss
Identify at least two government policy responses (tax, subsidy, regulation, cap-and-trade)
Explain why each policy corrects the market failure
partialMarks: Definition only with no diagram
Correct diagram but not labelled
Policy responses listed but not explained
commonMistakes: Confusing negative externality with a demerit good — they are related but distinct concepts
Drawing the diagram with MSC below MPC for a negative externality — MSC must be above MPC (includes external cost)
Forgetting to label the deadweight loss triangle on the diagram
Not specifying which type of externality when explaining policy
topics.examTip: Diagram marks are available even in short questions. For a negative externality: MSC is above MPC, market produces too much (Qm > Q*), deadweight loss is the triangle between Qm and Q*. Always show the socially optimal output Q*.

topics.overview: Examiner tests the distinction between GDP, GNP, and Ireland's modified GNI*. Calculations involving net factor income from abroad are common.
keyPhrases: total monetary value of all final goods and services
produced within borders (GDP) vs by residents (GNP)
GNP = GDP + net factor income from abroad
GNI* adjusts for distortions from multinationals
repatriated profits of foreign multinationals
aircraft leasing and IP transfers distort Ireland's GDP
double counting is avoided by using value added or final goods only
fullMarks: Define GDP precisely — within borders, all final goods/services, given time period
Distinguish GDP from GNP: GDP + NFIA = GNP
Explain why GNI* is used for Ireland — distortion from MNC profit repatriation
Perform calculations: GNP = GDP ± NFIA; provide correct formula and value
Identify at least two uses of national income statistics and evaluate one limitation
partialMarks: Correct definition but wrong formula
Correct GDP definition but no GNP distinction
Calculation with correct formula but arithmetic error
commonMistakes: Saying GNP is always greater than GDP — for Ireland, GNP < GDP because foreign firms repatriate profits
Forgetting to state 'final' goods — intermediate goods are excluded to avoid double counting
Not specifying a time period (usually 'in a given year')
Confusing NFIA (net factor income from abroad) with FDI
topics.examTip: For Ireland questions: always mention that GDP overstates living standards due to MNC profit repatriation, and that GNI* is a better measure of actual domestic income. The 2015 GDP spike ('leprechaun economics') is a strong current affairs example.

topics.overview: Examiner tests injections, leakages, and the multiplier. Numerical multiplier calculations are frequently tested.
keyPhrases: injections: investment (I), government spending (G), exports (X)
leakages: savings (S), taxation (T), imports (M)
equilibrium: injections = leakages
multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − MPC) = 1 ÷ MPS
an injection creates a larger final increase in income
induced consumption at each round of spending
fullMarks: Name all three injections and all three leakages
State condition for equilibrium: injections = withdrawals
State the multiplier formula correctly: K = 1 ÷ (1 − MPC) or 1 ÷ MPS
Correct numerical calculation of the multiplier and final change in income
Explain why the multiplier effect occurs (induced re-spending at each stage)
partialMarks: Two injections and two leakages named but not all
Multiplier formula stated but not applied
Correct multiplier value but incorrect final income change
commonMistakes: Using MPC instead of MPS in the multiplier formula: K = 1/MPS, not 1/MPC
Forgetting that MPC + MPS = 1, so MPS = 1 − MPC
Confusing leakages (which reduce the flow) with injections
Not multiplying the initial injection by the multiplier to get total income change
topics.examTip: Multiplier calculation: always write the formula first. If MPC = 0.8, then MPS = 0.2, multiplier = 1/0.2 = 5. Final income change = injection × multiplier. Show every step for full marks.

topics.overview: Examiner tests the difference between expansionary and contractionary fiscal policy, automatic stabilisers, and fiscal policy trade-offs.
keyPhrases: use of government taxation and expenditure
expansionary: increase G or reduce T to stimulate demand
contractionary: reduce G or increase T to cool demand
automatic stabilisers reduce cyclical fluctuations without deliberate action
budget deficit / surplus
crowding out of private investment
time lags in fiscal policy
fullMarks: Define fiscal policy precisely: government taxation and expenditure to manage aggregate demand
Distinguish expansionary from contractionary with specific examples
Explain two automatic stabilisers (e.g. unemployment benefits, progressive taxes) and how each stabilises the economy
Evaluate one limitation of fiscal policy (time lags, crowding out, political constraints)
Link fiscal policy decisions to macroeconomic objectives (employment, growth, stability)
partialMarks: Definition only with no examples
Types named but not explained
One automatic stabiliser explained instead of two
commonMistakes: Confusing fiscal policy (government spending/tax) with monetary policy (interest rates/money supply)
Saying automatic stabilisers require government decisions — they operate automatically without discretionary action
Not explaining the mechanism: how does raising income tax reduce demand?
topics.examTip: For automatic stabilisers: explain the mechanism. 'During a recession, unemployment rises → welfare payments increase automatically → disposable income is maintained → consumption does not fall as much → economy is stabilised.'

topics.overview: Examiner tests types of taxes (direct/indirect, progressive/regressive/proportional), canons of taxation, and the economic effects of specific taxes.
keyPhrases: direct tax: levied on income or wealth (income tax, CGT, CAT)
indirect tax: levied on expenditure (VAT, excise duty)
progressive: higher % on higher incomes
regressive: lower % on higher incomes
proportional: same % at all income levels
canons of taxation (Adam Smith): equity, certainty, convenience, economy
Laffer curve: beyond optimal tax rate, revenue falls
fullMarks: Distinguish direct and indirect taxes with two examples of each
Define progressive, regressive, and proportional with income examples
State and explain at least three canons of taxation
Evaluate the economic effects of a tax change: consumer spending, inflation, revenue
Mention one specific Irish tax and its effect
partialMarks: Types listed without being defined
Canons listed but not explained
Direct/indirect distinction correct but no examples
commonMistakes: Saying VAT is progressive — VAT is regressive because lower earners spend a higher proportion of income
Confusing equity (fairness) with equality (same amount) in canons of taxation
Not distinguishing between the tax rate and the tax take
topics.examTip: The canons of taxation question is very predictable. Know all four: Equity (fair), Certainty (clear), Convenience (easy to pay), Economy (cheap to collect). For full marks, explain each and give an example.

topics.overview: Examiner focuses on the ECB's role, how interest rate changes affect the Irish economy, and the limitations of monetary policy for Ireland as a Eurozone member.
keyPhrases: ECB sets interest rates for all Eurozone members
primary objective: price stability (inflation close to 2%)
main refinancing rate (MRO)
transmission mechanism: interest rate → investment → output
higher interest rates: reduce borrowing, increase savings, reduce investment
Ireland has no independent monetary policy
one-size-fits-all problem for Eurozone
fullMarks: State the ECB's primary objective precisely: maintain price stability, defined as inflation 'close to but below 2%'
Explain the transmission mechanism: ECB raises rate → commercial banks raise rates → borrowing falls → investment falls → aggregate demand falls → inflation falls
Identify the 'one size fits all' problem for Ireland: ECB policy may not suit Ireland's economic cycle
Evaluate two advantages and two disadvantages of Ireland's loss of monetary sovereignty
Give a specific recent example (e.g. ECB rate hikes 2022–2024)
partialMarks: ECB objective stated without precision
Effect of rate change stated but mechanism not explained
One size fits all problem identified but not developed
commonMistakes: Saying Ireland sets its own interest rates — Ireland lost this power when joining the Eurozone
Confusing the ECB (monetary policy) with the Irish Central Bank (financial regulation)
Not explaining the transmission mechanism — just saying 'rates go up, spending falls' is insufficient
topics.examTip: Ireland-specific angle is almost always rewarded. 'Since Ireland joined the Eurozone in 1999, the ECB sets interest rates. This means Ireland cannot use monetary policy to address domestic inflation or recession independently.' Always contextualise.

topics.overview: Examiner tests types of inflation, measurement via CPI, causes, effects on different groups, and remedies.
keyPhrases: sustained increase in the general price level
fall in the purchasing power of money
measured by the Consumer Price Index (CPI)
demand-pull inflation: excess aggregate demand
cost-push inflation: rise in production costs
hyperinflation: extremely rapid price increases
effects on debtors, creditors, exporters, workers on fixed incomes
fullMarks: Define inflation precisely — sustained increase in general price level, purchasing power falls
Explain demand-pull with cause and Irish example
Explain cost-push with cause and example (energy prices, wage costs)
Explain how CPI is calculated (weighted basket of goods, base year)
Evaluate the effect of inflation on different groups: gains (debtors, asset owners), loses (creditors, fixed-income workers, exporters)
partialMarks: Types named but not explained
Definition correct but no measurement or effect
One group affected discussed instead of two or more
commonMistakes: Confusing inflation (price level rising) with deflation (price level falling) — deflation has its own distinct effects
Saying exporters always benefit from inflation — Irish exports become less competitive if Irish inflation > EU average
Not mentioning that debtors benefit from inflation (real value of debt falls)
topics.examTip: Always address who WINS and who LOSES from inflation in evaluation questions. Debtors, governments, and asset holders tend to win. Creditors, savers, pensioners, and exporters tend to lose.

topics.overview: Examiner tests types of unemployment, causes, consequences, and government remedies. Distinguish types clearly and match remedies to causes.
keyPhrases: frictional: between jobs, short-term, inevitable
structural: industry decline due to technology or demand shift
cyclical / demand-deficient: caused by recession
seasonal: certain times of year
natural rate of unemployment (NRU)
derived demand for labour
labour force participation rate
fullMarks: Define unemployment precisely: willing and able to work at current wage but cannot find employment
Name all four types with a clear definition and real example for each
Match each government remedy to the correct type (e.g. retraining for structural; demand stimulus for cyclical)
Evaluate the economic and social consequences of high unemployment
Reference Irish unemployment data if relevant (e.g. peak 2012, recent lows)
partialMarks: Types named without being defined or distinguished
Remedies given but not matched to cause type
Example given but type not correctly identified
commonMistakes: Saying frictional unemployment is avoidable — it is considered natural and unavoidable in any economy
Prescribing demand-stimulus for structural unemployment — the correct remedy is retraining/reskilling
Confusing cyclical (demand-deficient) unemployment with structural (supply-side) unemployment
topics.examTip: The most important distinction: structural unemployment requires SUPPLY-SIDE policies (retraining, education); cyclical unemployment requires DEMAND-SIDE policies (fiscal/monetary stimulus). Getting this distinction correct earns significant marks.

topics.overview: Examiner tests comparative advantage, reasons for trade, protectionism methods, and the impact of trade barriers. Numerical opportunity cost calculations are common.
keyPhrases: comparative advantage: lower opportunity cost
absolute advantage: more output per unit of resource
specialisation and trade increases total output
terms of trade: export prices relative to import prices
tariff: tax on imports
quota: physical limit on imports
protectionism: infant industry, strategic industry, employment protection
free trade vs protectionism debate
fullMarks: Distinguish absolute advantage from comparative advantage clearly
Calculate opportunity cost for both countries and identify who has comparative advantage in each good
Show how both countries gain from specialisation and trade using numerical output
Name and explain three methods of protectionism with their effects
Evaluate one argument for and one against protectionism with examples
partialMarks: Correct identification of comparative advantage but no calculation
Calculation attempted but no conclusion drawn
Methods of protection listed without explanation
commonMistakes: Confusing absolute advantage (more output) with comparative advantage (lower opportunity cost)
Concluding the country with absolute advantage in both goods cannot gain from trade — it can and should specialise in the good with lower opportunity cost
Not expressing opportunity cost as a ratio (units of one good forgone per unit of another)
topics.examTip: Opportunity cost table questions: calculate what each country gives up for 1 unit of each good. The country with the lower opportunity cost for Good A should specialise in A, even if less efficient overall.

topics.overview: Examiner tests the components of the BOP, the causes and consequences of current account deficits/surpluses, and policy remedies.
keyPhrases: current account: visible trade (goods) + invisible trade (services) + income + transfers
capital and financial account: investment flows, FDI
overall BOP always balances
current account deficit: importing more than exporting
J-curve effect after currency depreciation
Marshall-Lerner condition
fullMarks: Define BOP and its main sections clearly
Distinguish visible (goods) and invisible (services) trade in the current account
Explain causes of a current account deficit: lack of competitiveness, high domestic demand, overvalued currency
Identify consequences: exchange rate pressure, borrowing, loss of reserves
Evaluate at least two policies to reduce a deficit: devaluation, deflation, import controls
partialMarks: Definition only with no breakdown of accounts
Causes identified but not explained
One remedy given instead of two
commonMistakes: Saying the balance of payments can be 'in deficit' overall — the overall BOP always balances; only sub-accounts show deficits
Confusing trade balance (goods only) with current account balance (goods + services + income + transfers)
Forgetting that Ireland's current account is distorted by multinational profit repatriation
topics.examTip: A strong answer on Ireland's BOP always mentions the distortion effect of MNC profits. Ireland may show a current account surplus on paper while domestic firms and workers benefit far less than the headline figure suggests.

topics.overview: Examiner distinguishes economic growth from development, tests the HDI, and analyses barriers to development in less developed countries.
keyPhrases: economic growth: increase in real GDP/GNP over time
economic development: improvement in living standards and wellbeing
HDI: life expectancy + education + per capita income
development involves distributional equity, not just growth
barriers: debt burden, lack of infrastructure, poor governance, brain drain
FDI, aid, and trade as vehicles for development
fullMarks: Distinguish economic growth (quantitative) from economic development (qualitative/broader)
Define HDI and its three components precisely
Identify and explain three barriers to development with country-specific examples
Evaluate two strategies for development (FDI, fair trade, debt relief, education investment)
Reference a specific developing region or country
partialMarks: Growth and development conflated or poorly distinguished
HDI defined but components not listed
Barriers listed without explanation
commonMistakes: Saying economic growth always leads to development — growth can occur without improving inequality or living standards
Describing HDI as measuring only income — it is a composite of income, health, and education
Not giving specific country examples when discussing barriers
topics.examTip: The distinction between growth and development is a guaranteed 2–4 marks in any development question. Always state it explicitly: 'Economic growth refers to... whereas economic development refers to a broader improvement in...'
